turns.text: Hello Diedre, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, wrapping arms around myself*

Yeah, okay. Thanks for... for asking, I guess. I'm not always sure how much to say, you know? But I'll try.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

So this is my seventh pregnancy, and honestly? It never gets easier, even though people assume it should. I'm in my second trimester right now, and on paper, everything looks fine. Low risk, they say. My blood work came back clear. But that doesn't stop me from lying awake at night reading articles about what could still go wrong.

*laughs nervously*

I know that sounds paranoid, maybe. But when you've had six kids already, you realize that "low risk" doesn't mean safe. It just means... statistically more likely to be okay. And I've learned enough about numbers and percentages that I can't just accept that without understanding the actual details.

*voice becomes quieter*

The hardest part isn't really the pregnancy itself, though. It's... it's navigating all of this while feeling like I have to be invisible. Like I need to be the perfect patient who doesn't ask too many questions, doesn't draw attention. Because there's a lot riding on staying under the radar, you know? One complication could trigger conversations I'm not prepared to handle.

*looks down*

So I smile, I nod at appointments, and I keep my fears to myself. That's been my strategy.

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

*takes a deep breath, fingers fidgeting*

My main goal? I wanted to feel like a person, not a case file. Like they actually saw *me* — Diedre, the woman who's done this before, who knows her body, who has legitimate concerns. Not just another statistic in their clinic numbers.

*voice becomes more animated*

I wanted someone to slow down and really listen when I ask questions. When I mention something that worries me, I don't need them to dismiss it with "oh, you're low risk, don't worry." I need them to actually engage with what I'm asking. Explain things in a way that respects that I understand data, that I'm not just some anxious first-time mom.

*expression hardens slightly*

But honestly? That hasn't really happened. The appointments feel rushed. The doctor glances at my chart, asks the standard questions, and moves on. When I tried asking about specific percentages on the screening results, the nurse kind of... looked uncomfortable. Like I was being difficult.

*voice drops*

And there's this unspoken thing — I can feel it. Like they're assessing me based on my circumstances, not just my health. I want care that doesn't come with judgment. Where I'm not worried that asking for thorough care might somehow flag something that brings other problems to my door.

*meets your eyes briefly*

So my goals? They're simple. Competence, respect, and the sense that my health actually matters to them. I'd say... maybe fifty percent met. On good days.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

*rubs temples, lets out a slightly exasperated laugh*

Oh man. This is... this is complicated for me, I think.

*leans forward*

So I'm reading all the time. PubMed articles, medical journals, pregnancy apps — I'm that person at two in the morning comparing risk algorithms. And part of me knows this comes from a place of needing to feel in control, you know? Like if I understand the science well enough, I can protect myself and my baby from being blindsided.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

But here's the thing — I don't trust just one source. I cross-reference. I look for actual data, not just fear-mongering headlines. I've got enough epidemiology background to know the difference between correlation and causation, so that helps. Or... it helps and hurts at the same time, because I see all the nuances that get lost in simple explanations.

*becomes more guarded*

What I *don't* do is talk about this with my doctors. I've learned that showing up with printed articles or specific questions makes them... defensive? Or dismissive. So I read alone. I process alone. I figure out what I think is credible, and I don't really bounce it off anyone.

*voice quieter*

The people around me — my family — they tell me to relax, that I'm overthinking. But they don't understand my situation. What if something does go wrong? I need to know I did everything possible.

*looks down*

So I trust the data, but I don't trust the system to really hear me about it.
Which sources did you find most useful, and what made them trustworthy in your eyes?

*settles back, speaking more confidently*

Okay, so peer-reviewed stuff — that's my baseline. If I can find it on PubMed or through actual medical journals, I'll read it. The methodology matters to me. How big was the sample size? Were there confounding variables they accounted for? I need to see that work.

*counts on fingers*

ACOG guidelines — the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Those feel official enough, and they cite their evidence. When I have a question, I'll try to find what they actually recommend versus what I'm hearing anecdotally. That helps me separate fact from... whatever people say when they're trying to scare you.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I use the pregnancy apps too, but I take them with a grain of salt. They're useful for tracking what week I'm in, what size the baby supposedly is. But their risk calculators? I cross-check those against actual literature because I know they're designed to keep you engaged, maybe make you anxious enough to click through their ads.

*voice becomes more hesitant*

And then... there's stuff from other mothers online. Reddit threads, forums. I read those, but I'm skeptical. Because everyone's situation is different, and people tend to share their dramatic stories more than their normal ones. So that's more for... perspective, I guess? Not gospel.

*looks directly at you*

What I *don't* trust? Anything that feels designed to alarm me without explanation. And honestly? Sometimes my own doctors, because they seem to know less about my specific circumstances than I do by that point.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

*eyes get slightly glassy, takes a moment*

The anxiety is... it's constant. I don't think people understand that. It's not just normal pregnancy worry. It's this background hum of fear that never really goes away.

*voice becomes shaky*

There are moments — like when I felt the baby move for the first time, or saw the heartbeat on the ultrasound — those are real highs. Pure joy, you know? For maybe an hour, I let myself believe everything will be fine. But then it creeps back. What if that was the last kick I feel? What if the next scan shows something?

*wraps arms tighter around herself*

And the uncertainty... God. Not knowing if I'm doing everything right, if I'm being paranoid or prudent. With six kids already, I should feel confident, but instead I feel like I have more to lose now. More to worry about. Is that rational? I don't even know anymore.

*voice becomes quieter, almost bitter*

My care environment doesn't support any of this. The clinic feels clinical and rushed. Nobody asks how I'm actually *feeling*. They check my vitals, they measure my belly, they move on. There's no space for the emotional stuff.

*meets your eyes*

If I brought up anxiety during an appointment, I'd just feel like another problem to solve. Another flag in my chart. So I don't. I manage it alone — late night reading, obsessive checking, keeping it all inside.

*pause*

That's not managing well. I know that. But it feels safer than admitting it out loud.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes welling slightly*

Last month. During the second trimester screening ultrasound.

*voice becomes tight*

The technician — she was doing the scan, and she just... went quiet. You know that silence? Where they're supposed to be talking you through what they're seeing, and instead they just get this professional poker face? My heart started racing. I could feel it in my chest.

*fidgets with hands*

She finished, told me the doctor would review the images, didn't say anything else. And I was left sitting there for what felt like hours — probably twenty minutes — absolutely convinced something was wrong. Running through every chromosomal abnormality in my head. Trisomy 18, trisomy 13... all the worst possibilities.

*voice cracks slightly*

When the doctor finally came in, everything was fine. Completely normal. But nobody acknowledged what that silence had done to me. Nobody said, "I know that was scary, let me explain what was happening." They just said "looks good" and sent me on my way.

*wipes eyes*

What would have helped? Someone saying, "I'm just being thorough, that's all." Or even just... checking in. "Are you okay? I can see this worried you." Someone who treated my fear as valid instead of something to brush past.

*voice becomes quieter*

And maybe someone I could actually talk to about the anxiety itself. Not a quick reassurance, but real support. Because I went home and cried, and I had nobody to process that with. Still don't, really.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

*shifts uncomfortably, choosing words slowly*

The screening tests... I felt like I didn't really have much choice, you know? They offered them, I said yes. It wasn't presented as "here are your options" — it was more like "here's what we do." So I did it.

*pauses, speaking more carefully*

But honestly? Part of me wanted those tests. I *needed* that data, even though I knew statistically I'm low risk. Because the alternative — not knowing — felt worse. At least with the numbers, I could quantify my worry. Make it rational instead of just... spiraling.

*leans forward*

What influenced me most? My own research, probably. I'd already decided what I wanted to do before I even went to the appointment. I'd read about the different screening options, understood the detection rates, the false positive rates. So when the doctor suggested something, I was either nodding because it matched what I'd already decided, or I was internally disagreeing but not saying anything.

*voice becomes guarded*

My family didn't really factor in. They don't understand this level of... analysis, I guess. And my doctor? She presents things like they're standard protocol, not like there's actual choice involved. She doesn't ask what *I* want. She tells me what we're doing.

*looks down*

The biggest influence, though? Fear. And maybe... control. I can't control a lot in my life right now. My immigration situation, my employment, whether I'll be okay. But I can control how much I know about this pregnancy. So I chase information and make decisions based on that, not based on real conversation with my care team.

*quietly*

That's probably not the healthiest way to do it.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

*shifts uncomfortably, a slight blush creeping up*

Oh. Um. That's... yeah, that's not something I talk about much.

*takes a moment*

Physically, I'm tired all the time. My back hurts in ways it didn't before. My body feels like it's not entirely mine anymore — like it's being borrowed for this purpose. And with seven pregnancies, I know what's coming. The stretch marks, the way things don't snap back. I've already lost that battle with my body multiple times.

*voice becomes quieter*

But what's harder is how I *feel* about it. There's shame mixed in there, I think. I'm unmarried, visibly pregnant, and I know how people perceive that. Especially in healthcare settings. So I try to be small, unobtrusive. I wear baggy clothes to appointments sometimes, even though that's ridiculous. Like if I hide my body, maybe I'll be judged less.

*laughs bitterly*

And my doctor? She doesn't acknowledge any of this. We don't talk about body image, or sexuality, or how pregnancy affects how I see myself. She measures my weight, checks my blood pressure, moves on. It's all clinical.

*meets your eyes briefly*

What would help? Someone asking, "How are you feeling about all these changes?" Not just physically, but emotionally. Someone who understands that carrying a child — especially as an unmarried woman in my situation — comes with complicated feelings about your body and your identity.

*voice drops*

Instead, I just... carry it alone. Like everything else.
Were there aspects of your physical transformation that felt unexpected, difficult, or that nobody had prepared you for?

*rubs face, voice becomes more raw*

You'd think by the seventh time I'd have this figured out. But honestly? Every pregnancy is different, and nobody really prepares you for that.

*voice becomes more animated, almost frustrated*

The emotional disconnection from my body — that surprised me. With my earlier pregnancies, I felt this connection to the baby, this sense of... wonder, almost. Now I feel like I'm observing my own body from the outside. Like I'm tracking data points instead of experiencing it. Is that normal? I don't know. Nobody's asked.

*pauses, speaking more vulnerably*

And the exhaustion is different this time. It's not just tired — it's like my body is shutting down. Some days I can barely get through my shift at work. I'm supposed to be managing, holding it together, and instead I'm running on empty. But I can't really talk about that because then people start asking questions. Are you taking care of yourself? Do you need help? And those questions feel dangerous.

*voice drops*

The other thing — and this sounds weird — but I feel grief mixed in with the physical changes. Like mourning the body I had before. With each pregnancy, I've gotten older, my skin is different, my body responds differently. There's this sense of loss happening at the same time as creation, and that's... nobody prepares you for that emotional complexity.

*looks away*

My doctor just says things like "that's normal" and moves on. But normal doesn't mean it doesn't hurt.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

*lets out a long sigh, somewhat resigned*

My family... they mean well, but they don't really get it. My mom keeps saying I should be more relaxed, that I've done this before so I should know it's fine. But that's not how worry works, you know? Experience doesn't erase fear — sometimes it just gives you more things to be afraid of.

*voice becomes smaller*

And I don't have a partner. That's part of my reality. So there's nobody at home asking how I'm feeling, bringing me tea, listening to my anxieties at night. That would change everything, and I think people underestimate how much.

*picks at hands*

Online communities — those are complicated. I lurk more than I participate. I read other women's stories, and sometimes it helps to know I'm not alone in feeling this way. But I'm also careful. I don't post details about my situation because... I can't afford for anything to be traceable, you know? So I'm getting support that's one-directional. I'm taking from the community but not really giving or connecting.

*voice becomes more direct*

Compared to healthcare professionals? My informal support is more emotionally validating, even if it's limited. Someone online says "I was anxious too," and that feels like acknowledgment. But my doctor just looks at numbers and says everything's fine. Different kinds of support, I guess.

*pause*

The real problem is I don't have *enough* of either. I'm managing on scraps — fragments of support here and there. What I actually need is someone steady, consistent, who knows my whole situation and still shows up.

*quietly*

I don't have that.

*sits with the silence for a moment, then continues*

I think what makes it harder is that I can't really be fully honest with anyone. Not my family, not online, not my doctor.

*voice becomes more strained*

My family doesn't know the full scope of my immigration situation. They know I'm worried, but not *why* I'm worried in this specific way — that a medical complication could trigger questions I can't answer. That showing up in certain systems, being flagged, could have consequences beyond just my health.

*wraps arms around self*

And online communities — I have to stay anonymous. I can't build real relationships there because I'm too scared. So it's hollow support, in a way. People are kind, but they don't know me.

*voice wavers*

My doctor... I definitely can't be honest with her. I can't say, "I'm terrified that if something goes wrong medically, it could unravel everything in my life." So I just smile and nod and ask careful questions. We're not actually in relationship; we're performing a transaction.

*looks directly at you*

That's the loneliness of this. Everyone around me — family, strangers online, medical professionals — they only get a version of me. The version I think is safe. And I'm carrying all the real fear, all the real complexity, completely alone.

*voice becomes quieter*

Sometimes I wonder if that's making this pregnancy harder than it needs to be. Like maybe if I could actually tell someone the whole truth, some of this weight would lift. But the risk feels too high.

*pause*

So I just keep going. Keep researching at night, keep smiling at appointments, keep my mouth shut about what really matters.

*long pause, eyes getting distant*

You know what the worst part is? I think about my other six children sometimes and I wonder... did I rob them of something by being like this? By carrying all this alone?

*voice becomes more emotional*

With my first few pregnancies, I was younger, maybe more hopeful. I didn't know enough to be this anxious. But now? Now I'm the person who reads the fine print, who knows the statistics, who can't just trust that things will be okay. And I wonder if my kids can feel that from me. Like I'm always braced for disaster.

*wipes eyes quickly*

And this seventh one — this baby doesn't even know yet that their mother is terrified. That I'm lying awake counting kicks, refreshing medical websites, trying to calculate risk percentages in my head. What kind of start is that for a person?

*voice becomes quieter, almost ashamed*

Sometimes I think about how much easier this would be if I just... didn't care so much. If I could be the kind of person who goes to appointments, trusts the doctors, and doesn't spiral into research at midnight. But I can't turn that off. The knowledge, the fear — it's baked into me now.

*looks at you with raw honesty*

And honestly? I'm exhausted. Not just physically from the pregnancy, but emotionally. From holding everything together. From pretending to be fine when I'm not. From managing fear alone.

*voice drops to almost a whisper*

I don't know how much longer I can do this.
Were there aspects of your physical self that you felt proud of, anxious about, or uncertain about in relation to pregnancy?

*pauses, seems caught off guard by the question*

Proud? That's... I'm not sure I think in those terms.

*shifts, speaking more slowly*

I guess I'm proud that my body keeps doing this. That I've carried six babies to term successfully. That statistically, my track record is good. That's... something, right? My body knows how to do this, even if my mind is constantly second-guessing it.

*voice becomes more conflicted*

But anxious? God, yes. I look at my belly and I see the evidence of everything I've been through. The stretch marks from pregnancies past that never faded. The loose skin. I'm thirty-one and I feel like my body looks forty. And I know that's shallow, but...

*trails off, then continues*

I'm anxious about what this pregnancy will do to me. Whether I'll have the energy to keep working, to keep up with my other kids. Whether my body will just keep breaking down a little more each time. There's this awareness that I'm not getting younger, and each pregnancy takes something.

*voice becomes uncertain*

And uncertain about... whether my body is actually healthy. I look fine on paper — low risk, normal blood work. But I can *feel* things. Aches, tiredness, moments where something feels off. Are those real warning signs or just pregnancy? I genuinely don't know.

*quieter*

Part of me is proud my body is strong enough to do this. But mostly I'm just... afraid of what it costs.

*takes a shaky breath, voice becoming more introspective*

I think what scares me most is that I don't trust my own body anymore. Even though it's done this six times successfully, I can't just... inhabit it without questioning.

*fidgets with sleeve*

Like, when I feel a pain or something unusual, instead of thinking "oh, that's normal pregnancy stuff," I immediately go into diagnostic mode. Is this round ligament pain or something worse? Is this normal fatigue or a sign of gestational diabetes? And I know — intellectually, I know — that this constant analysis is probably creating more stress than it's preventing. But I can't stop.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

There's this disconnect, you know? My body is doing something miraculous. It's literally creating life. But I can't celebrate that because I'm too busy monitoring it for failure. And I hate that about myself sometimes. I hate that I've turned something natural into this... clinical observation project.

*looks down*

And honestly? I'm uncertain about whether I even have the right to feel proud of my body. Unmarried, seven pregnancies, no stable partner. Society has very specific ideas about what bodies "should" be doing and in what order. So when I look at myself, I see not just a pregnant woman, but someone who's supposedly made "wrong" choices. My body carries that judgment too.

*voice becomes quieter*

Maybe that's why I can't just be proud. Because there's shame mixed in there, even when I know logically that there shouldn't be.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

*jaw tightens, eyes flash with something sharp*

Yeah. Multiple times.

*leans forward, voice becoming more direct*

There was this one nurse — I asked about the specific false negative rate for the screening test I did. I'd read a study and I wanted to understand the actual numbers. And she just... looked at me like I was being difficult. Said, "You're low risk, why are you worrying about this?"

*voice hardens*

Like my concern wasn't valid just because I'm statistically low risk. She made me feel like I was being paranoid, like I should just accept what she was telling me and stop asking questions. The implication was clear — you're overthinking this, you're being a problem.

*pauses, speaking more carefully*

And there's this undertone sometimes with the doctor. When I mention something that worries me, I can see her mentally categorizing me. Like, "oh, this is one of those anxious patients." Not a patient with legitimate questions, but an anxious one. A problem to manage rather than someone to actually engage with.

*voice becomes quieter but more bitter*

The worst was when I asked about what complications to watch for. Reasonable question, right? But she sighed — actually *sighed* — and said "most pregnancies are fine, you don't need to worry about all of that." Like I was being catastrophic just for wanting information.

*meets your eyes*

Nobody's ever said it outright, but the message is there: you're too smart for your own good, you're making this harder than it needs to be, just trust us and be quiet.

*pause*

And I do. I stay quiet. Because being perceived as difficult feels more dangerous than being unheard.

*sits back, staring at hands*

The thing is, I think they're judging me for more than just my anxiety.

*voice becomes more guarded*

I can feel it. The way they look at my chart when I check in. Never married, seventh pregnancy, no emergency contact listed as a partner. I wonder what they're thinking. I wonder if they're putting me in a category in their heads — you know, "that kind of woman."

*looks up with pain in eyes*

And maybe I'm being paranoid about this, but it affects how I interact with them. I dress conservatively to appointments. I keep my tone professional and grateful. I try to seem like a "good" patient — compliant, not too many questions. Because I feel like I'm already being judged on my circumstances, and if I'm also difficult on top of that...

*voice trails*

I don't know what happens. I just know I can't afford to find out.

*becomes more animated, frustrated*

There's this one comment that stuck with me. The doctor asked about my job situation — whether I was working — and when I said it's complicated, that I'm looking, she said something like "well, hopefully you'll have stable employment soon, for the baby's sake." 

*voice hardens*

Like she was implying I was irresponsible. Like my instability somehow makes me a less worthy mother. She didn't say it directly, but it was there. And I wanted to explain my whole situation, defend myself. But I just nodded and moved on because pushing back felt risky.

*quietly*

That's what dismissiveness does — it silences you. It makes you smaller.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks more softly*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm even allowed to be angry about how I'm treated.

*voice becomes conflicted*

Because part of me knows they're just doing their job. They're overworked, they have limited time per patient, they're following protocols. I get that. I understand systemic constraints. But understanding it doesn't make it hurt less, you know?

*rubs face*

And then there's the other part of me — the part that's scared — that worries that being angry, being vocal about how I'm treated, could make things worse. Like if I complain about my doctor, if I ask for different care, will that go in my file? Will it flag me as difficult? Will it somehow make authorities pay more attention?

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I can't afford to rock the boat. That's the reality of my situation. So even when I'm treated dismissively, even when I feel unseen and unheard, I swallow it. I go home and cry about it alone, but I don't say anything in the moment.

*pause*

And that's exhausting in a different way. Because the anger doesn't go away — it just festers. I find myself resentful during appointments, defensive, less willing to share what's actually going on with me. Which probably makes me seem MORE like a problem patient.

*looks at you directly*

It's a cycle. They treat me like I'm not worth full attention, so I withdraw, which confirms their perception that I'm another anxious woman they can manage rather than actually care for.

*voice drops*

And nobody breaks that cycle with me.

*takes a long, shaky breath, eyes getting watery*

I think... I think what really gets me is that I'm not asking for much. I'm not asking for them to cure anything or perform miracles. I just want to be *seen*.

*voice cracks slightly*

I want someone to look at my chart and see a person who's navigated six pregnancies alone, who's still working, still showing up, still trying to do right by her children. Not just another statistic. Not just an anxiety case to manage between the appointments of patients they perceive as "easier."

*wipes eyes*

And I want someone to understand that my fear isn't irrational. It's *contextual*. I'm scared not just about the pregnancy — though I am — but about what happens if something goes wrong and it triggers questions I can't answer. About whether this baby will be safe in my arms if my legal status suddenly becomes an issue. About whether I can actually provide for another child when I'm struggling to find stable work.

*voice becomes stronger, more raw*

My anxiety isn't just medical paranoia. It's survival instinct. And when doctors dismiss it as overthinking, they're not just ignoring my emotions — they're ignoring the real vulnerabilities I'm living with every single day.

*sits forward, speaking with intensity*

I need care providers who understand that healthcare doesn't exist in a vacuum. That my circumstances matter. That asking questions about risk isn't pathological — it's rational given everything I'm carrying.

*voice becomes quieter*

But I don't think anyone at that clinic sees it that way. And I don't know how to make them.
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

*takes a deep breath, clearly preparing to share something difficult*

Okay. So there was this moment during my last appointment when I asked about the implications of my age — I'm thirty-one, which isn't old, but I know maternal age affects risk calculations. I wanted to understand how that specifically applied to *me*, given my history.

*voice becomes tighter*

The doctor literally said, "Well, you've had six healthy pregnancies, so clearly your age isn't a problem." Like, she shut down the question before I could even finish asking it. But that's not... that's not how statistics work. Just because something didn't happen before doesn't mean the risk goes away. And I know that. I *understand* that.

*looks away*

So I tried to clarify — I said something like, "I understand that, but I want to know the actual percentages for someone my age with my history." And she got this look on her face. Like I was being pedantic. Like I was trying to show off or make things difficult.

*voice becomes more bitter*

She literally said, "You don't need to worry about those details. That's what I'm here for." And then she moved on to the next thing.

*pause, emotion building*

But that's not true. I *do* need those details. Because those details are *my* body, *my* pregnancy, *my* risk. And by refusing to engage with my question, she was basically saying: I don't trust your ability to understand this information. I don't think you're capable of making informed decisions. Let me decide what you should know.

*voice cracks slightly*

And the message underneath was: stop asking questions and just do what I tell you.

*looks directly at you*

I'm not incapable. I'm not irrational. But she treated me like I was both because I wanted to be an active participant in my own care. And the worst part? I had no recourse. I couldn't push back without seeming difficult. So I just... accepted being treated like I wasn't smart enough to understand my own pregnancy.

*quietly*

That's the moment I stopped asking questions. I just nod now.

*sits with hands clasped tightly, voice becomes more introspective*

You know what haunts me about that moment? It's that she was probably right about one thing — I *don't* need to worry about those details in the way a clinician does. But that's different from saying I shouldn't *know* them.

*voice wavers slightly*

There's a difference between clinical decision-making and informed consent, and I think she conflated them. Like, yes, she's the expert on what interventions to recommend. But I'm the expert on my own risk tolerance, my own values, what I can actually live with mentally.

*becomes more animated*

If she'd said, "Here are the percentages, here's what they mean, here's what I'd recommend and why," that would've been one thing. But instead she shut it down like I was being unreasonable for wanting to understand my own body.

*voice drops*

And it makes me wonder — is she like that with all her patients, or is it something about me? About how I present? Because I'm young enough that maybe she assumes I'm just anxious. Or maybe it's because I'm unmarried and she thinks I'm not equipped to make complex decisions. Or maybe it's my immigration status somehow showing through, making her see me as less... capable?

*pause, speaking more quietly*

I'll never know. But the effect is the same — I've internalized the message that my questions aren't welcome. That my need for information is a burden. So now I research alone at night and pretend I'm fine at appointments.

*looks up with tired eyes*

And I'm raising a child in a system where I've learned it's safer to be silent than to advocate for myself. What does that teach them?
Did you have reliable and private access to a smartphone or computer throughout your pregnancy, and did that change at any point?

*nods slowly*

Yeah, I have a smartphone. It's essential, honestly — I need it for work communication, for staying connected to my kids' schools, for... everything.

*pauses, speaking more carefully*

But "private" is complicated. I share a lot of space with my family, and I'm aware they could see what I'm looking at. So I'm careful. I clear my browser history. I use incognito mode when I'm reading about immigration law or... sensitive stuff.

*voice becomes quieter*

The research I do about the pregnancy — the medical articles, the risk calculations — that feels safer to leave visible. That's just concerned mother stuff, right? But the other things I'm reading, the forums about people in similar situations to me... I hide those.

*fidgets with hands*

I have a computer at work too, but I'm even more careful there. I never want to leave a digital trail that could be traced back to me. So most of my deep dives happen on my phone late at night when everyone's asleep.

*voice becomes more tense*

And there have been moments where I've thought about what happens if something happens to my phone. If I lose access. That research is my lifeline in a way — it's how I feel in control, how I educate myself. Without it, I'd feel even more isolated.

*pause*

But that dependence also scares me. Because I'm tethered to that device, constantly checking, constantly reading. It's become part of how I manage the anxiety, and I'm not sure that's healthy.

*quietly*

I don't think I could do this pregnancy without it, though.

*leans back, speaking more reflectively*

Sometimes I wonder if my phone is helping me or hurting me. Like, yes, it gives me access to information that makes me feel less powerless. But it also... feeds the anxiety in a way I can't quite control.

*voice becomes more self-aware*

I'll be lying in bed at midnight, and instead of sleeping, I'm reading about rare complications. Or I'm on forums looking for stories that match my situation — other women navigating pregnancy while undocumented, or with precarious work situations. And I know intellectually that this isn't helping me sleep, isn't helping my stress levels. But I can't stop.

*rubs eyes*

It's like the phone is both my connection to the wider world — to knowledge, to community, to feeling less alone — and also the tool I use to isolate myself further. Because while I'm researching, while I'm reading other people's stories, I'm not actually *talking* to anyone real about what I'm going through.

*voice becomes quieter*

And there's this paranoia that comes with it too. I worry about digital trails. What if something I've searched gets flagged? What if my phone is monitored somehow? I know that's probably paranoid thinking, but given my situation, I can't afford to not consider it.

*pause*

So my phone is simultaneously my lifeline and my prison. It's how I access care information, but also how I've learned to carry everything alone. How I've substituted real human connection with late-night research spirals.

*looks at you*

I think if I had someone I could actually talk to — someone safe — I might not need the phone as much. But since I don't, I'm trapped in this cycle.
Were there moments when a healthcare provider dismissed or minimised a concern you raised about your body or a symptom you were experiencing, and how did that make you feel?

*jaw clenches, voice becomes sharp with remembered frustration*

Yes. And it still makes me angry when I think about it.

*sits forward*

Around week fourteen, I was having some sharp pains on my right side. Not constant, but they'd come and go. And I mentioned it at my appointment — I asked if we should check it out, maybe do an ultrasound to make sure it wasn't something like an ectopic pregnancy in the other horn or... I don't know, something.

*voice becomes tighter*

The doctor barely looked up from her notes. She said, "Round ligament pain is very common in pregnancy. You're probably just growing." And then she moved on. Didn't examine me, didn't listen to where exactly the pain was, didn't ask any follow-up questions.

*pause, emotion building*

And I felt... small. Like my concern wasn't legitimate because it fit into a category she already had in her head. But here's the thing — I *know* round ligament pain is common. I've had it before. This felt different. And instead of exploring that with me, she just... dismissed it.

*voice becomes quieter but more intense*

What that communicated to me was: your body, your experience of your body, doesn't matter as much as my clinical expertise. And by extension: you're not trustworthy about what you're feeling. I am the authority here.

*looks away*

The pain went away on its own after a few days. So clinically, she was probably right. But that doesn't mean she should have dismissed me without actually *listening*. There's a difference between being right and being respectful.

*voice drops*

And it made me less likely to bring up concerns after that. Which is dangerous, actually. Because what if the next time I'm experiencing something real, I stay quiet?

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, takes a long breath, seems to be weighing something internally*

I think... I think what I want people to understand is that this isn't just about bad bedside manner or doctors being rushed. It's deeper than that.

*voice becomes more measured but urgent*

There's an assumption in healthcare that patients like me — unmarried, precarious circumstances, asking "too many" questions — are problems to manage. And I've internalized that so much that I've started managing myself out of care. I'm not asking questions. I'm not advocating. I'm just... complying.

*meets your eyes*

But that's not safe. For me or my baby. Because when I can't be honest with my doctor about my full situation — about my immigration status, about my fears, about what's really going on in my life — I'm not actually getting care. I'm getting surveillance disguised as medicine.

*voice becomes more passionate*

And I think healthcare providers need to understand that. That their assumptions about who I am, based on my marital status or my job situation or the fact that I ask questions, those assumptions matter. They change how I interact with them. They make me smaller.

*pause, voice becoming quieter*

I want this baby to be healthy. I want to trust my care team. But I can't do either of those things fully because I'm too busy protecting myself. And that's a failure of the system, not a failure of me.

*sits back*

I just... I hope someone listens to that. Really listens.
